name: i-Constructibility (Strict Grammar)
statement: i = sqrt(-1) is NOT constructible as a finite EML tree from terminal {1} under strict principal-branch grammar (ln(0) undefined, ln of negative reals returns complex via principal branch).
evidence: Exhaustive enumeration of all full binary EML trees with N=1..9 internal nodes (1429 trees total), all leaves assigned value 1. No tree evaluates to i. The loophole (negative y-argument entering ln) first activates at N=5, producing complex values with Im = -π. Subsequent compositions produce Im parts that are NOT integer multiples of π (Im/π census: irrational values like -0.48, -0.93, -1.59 appear). Despite the complex values, Im = 1 never appears.
proof_gap: Attempted iπ-closure induction: first complex generation has Im = -π (valid). But exp(a + ib) for general b breaks the integer-π invariant. A complete proof requires either: (a) a different algebraic invariant, (b) extending the search to N=11+ with the Rust kernel, or (c) a transcendence argument about which Im values are reachable.
verify:
python python/experiments/i_constructibility.py  # N=1..7
python python/experiments/i_constructibility_s62.py  # N=1..9 census

search_summary.note: N=12 Rust search running in background (started S61)

next_steps: Extend Python search to N=11 using Rust kernel
Explore transcendence argument: what algebraic structure do reachable Im values have?
